params.text:
The Chain of Survival

Survival from cardiac arrest depends on a series of linked actions happening quickly, one after another. Resuscitation Council UK describes this as the Chain of Survival. Each link relies on the ones before it — the chain is only as strong as its weakest link.

  • Early recognition and call for help — spot the emergency and call 999 to get the ambulance service on its way.
  • Early CPR — start chest compressions straight away to buy time and keep blood moving to the brain.
  • Early defibrillation — use an automated external defibrillator (AED) as soon as possible to try to restart a normal heart rhythm.
  • Post-resuscitation care — expert treatment by paramedics and in hospital to give the best chance of a full recovery.

params.title: Tap a card to flip it
params.description: The four links in the Chain of Survival. Tap each card to reveal why that link matters.

dialogs.text: Link 1: Early recognition and call for help
dialogs.answer: Recognising cardiac arrest early and calling 999 immediately gets skilled help on the way and stops precious minutes being lost.
Starting chest compressions straight away keeps oxygen-carrying blood flowing to the brain and buys time until a defibrillator arrives.
Using an AED quickly can shock the heart back into a normal rhythm. The chance of survival falls with every minute of delay.
Once the heart is restarted, expert care from paramedics and hospital teams gives the person the best chance of a good recovery.
